draft.introduction: The Testament of Levi is part of the Testaments of the Twelve Patriarchs, a Jewish text with likely Christian interpolations, composed in Greek between the second century BCE and the second century CE. Each patriarch gives a deathbed speech to his sons. Levi’s testament is the longest and most theologically ambitious, as befits the ancestor of the priestly line. Chapter 18 is the climactic prophecy: Levi foresees a “new priest” whom God will raise up in the last days. This figure will open the gates of paradise, remove the threatening sword, give the saints to eat from the tree of life, bind Beliar, and grant his children the authority to trample evil spirits. Whether this new priest is a messianic figure, a reference to the Qumran Teacher of Righteousness, or a Christian interpolation pointing to Christ is hotly debated. What is beyond dispute is that the passage describes a human being who exercises divine prerogatives—opening paradise, bestowing immortality, defeating cosmic evil. This is deification language, applied to a priestly figure.

translation.text: And after their punishment shall have come from the Lord, the priesthood shall fail. Then shall the Lord raise up a new priest, to whom all the words of the Lord shall be revealed. And he shall execute a judgment of truth upon the earth for a multitude of days.
translation.original_text: καὶ μετὰ τὴν εκδίκησιν αὐτῶν παρὰ Κυρίου, ἐκλείψει ἡ ἱερωσύνη. τότε ἀναστήσει Κύριος ἱερέα καινόν

translation.text: And his star shall arise in heaven, as a king shedding forth the light of knowledge in the sunshine of day, and he shall be magnified in the world. He shall shine forth as the sun in the earth, and shall remove all darkness from under heaven, and there shall be peace in all the earth.
translation.original_text: καὶ τὸ ἄστρον αὐτοῦ ἀνατελεῖ ἐν οὐρανῷ ὡς βασιλεύς

translation.text: The heavens shall be opened, and from the temple of glory shall come upon him sanctification, with the Father’s voice, as from Abraham to Isaac. And the glory of the Most High shall be uttered over him, and the spirit of understanding and sanctification shall rest upon him in the water.
translation.original_text: οἱ οὐρανοὶ ἀνοιγήσονται, καὶ ἐκ τοῦ ναοῦ τῆς δόξης ἥξει ἐπ’ αὐτὸν ἁγιασμός

translation.text: For he shall give the majesty of the Lord to his sons in truth forevermore. And there shall none succeed him for all generations forever. And in his priesthood the Gentiles shall be multiplied in knowledge upon the earth, and enlightened through the grace of the Lord.
translation.original_text: αὐτὸς γὰρ δώσει τὴν μεγαλωσύνην Κυρίου τοῖς υἱοῖς αὐτοῦ

translation.text: In his priesthood sin shall come to an end, and the lawless shall cease to do evil. And he shall open the gates of paradise, and shall remove the threatening sword against Adam. And he shall give to the saints to eat from the tree of life, and the spirit of holiness shall be on them.
translation.original_text: ἐν τῇ ἱερατείᾳ αὐτοῦ ἡ ἁμαρτία ἐκλείψει, καὶ οἱ ἄνομοι παύσονται κακοποιεῖν

translation.text: And Beliar shall be bound by him, and he shall give power to his children to tread upon evil spirits. And the Lord shall rejoice in his children, and be well pleased in his beloved ones forever. Then shall Abraham and Isaac and Jacob exult, and I will be glad, and all the saints shall clothe themselves with joy.
translation.original_text: καὶ δώσει τοῖς ἁγίοις φαγεῖν ἐκ τοῦ ξύλου τῆς ζωῆς

translator_notes.note: The heavens opening, the Father’s voice, and the spirit descending “in the water” clearly echo the Synoptic accounts of Jesus’ baptism (Mark 1:10-11 and parallels). Most scholars consider this section a Christian interpolation into an originally Jewish text. However, the core prophecy of a new priest may be genuinely pre-Christian, reflecting the same messianic priestly expectations found at Qumran.

The opening of paradise and the restoration of the tree of life reverse the expulsion from Eden in Genesis 3:24. This is a theme found across Second Temple literature: the eschaton is the restoration of Eden. The fact that a human priest performs these acts—opening paradise, removing the angelic sword, feeding the saints from the tree of life—places him in a role that is functionally divine.

The binding of Beliar (the chief evil spirit) echoes the binding of Azazel in 1 Enoch and anticipates the binding of Satan in Revelation 20. The power given to “children” to tread upon evil spirits echoes Luke 10:19. The final vision of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob exulting alongside the saints suggests a final communion of all the righteous—a corporate theosis.
